content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 2 Part 3 - The buyer decision process for new products.
Chapter 5 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 2 – Part 3 - The buyer decision process for new products
5.3 Describe the adoption and diffusion process for new products.

Part 3  - The buyer decision process for new products

The buyer decision process for new products (1 of 4)
Stages in the adoption process
The adoption process is the mental process through which an individual passes from first hearing about an innovation to final adoption.
Consumers go through five stages in the process of adopting a new product:
awareness
interest
evaluation
trial
adoption.
Q: Describe activities/techniques a marketer could employ to help consumers move through this process to adoption of their product.

The buyer decision process for new products (2 of 4)
Individual differences in innovativeness
People can be classified into five groups of adopters:
innovators – are venturesome
early adopters – are opinion leaders in their communities
early majority – are rarely leaders but adopt new ideas before the average person
late majority – are sceptical
laggards – are tradition-bound.
Q: Which of these adopter categories best describes you? Does ‘your’  category vary?

The buyer decision process for new products (3 of 4)
Figure 5.6
Adopter categorisation on the basis of relative time of adoption of innovations

The buyer decision process for new products (4 of 4)
Influence of product characteristic on rate of adoption
Five characteristics influence an innovation’s rate of adoption:
relative advantage – the degree to which the innovation appears superior to existing products
compatibility – the degree to which the innovation fits the values and experiences of potential consumers
complexity – the degree to which the innovation is difficult to understand or use
divisibility – the degree to which the innovation may be tried on a limited basis
communicability – the degree to which the results of using the innovation can be observed or described to others.

The buyer decision process for new products
Promote to Early Adopters so that they will make others aware of the new product and think that “people in the know” are using it.
Make sure that the user instructions ensure that first-time users get the best results first time – so that they will tell others that the new product is easy to use and delivers the promised results.
List the key criteria that the consumer uses to evaluate new products in this category.
Reduce risk of trial – offer a “low introductory trial price”, emphasise the guarantee, perhaps a “Money back guarantee”.
Have someone trustworthy promote the product such as a trusted celebrity, or someone known to be an expert in the product area, or a doctor or dentist (if a medical or dental product).
Q: Describe activities/techniques a marketer could employ to help consumers move through this process to adoption of their product. Give some examples of post-purchase behaviour.

The buyer decision process for new products
Usually, I am a Late Majority adopter, because I want to be sure that the “bugs” have been ironed-out of the new product, and because the price of a new product falls as it becomes less “new”. And because I am a naturally conservative person and frugal – I want to use whatever I have now until it wears ours out.
However, I am an Early Adopter in product categories in which I am very interested; in my case any new spy or detective series on the streaming services. Firstly, because I have seen most of the old ones and secondly because I am always interested in the new ones.
Q: Which of these adopter categories best describes you? Does ‘your’  category vary?

Learning Objective 3– now you have learnt how to:
5.3 Describe the adoption and diffusion process for new products.
